title: Unified Payments Interface (UPI) and NPCI
focus: Features of UPI, its international expansion via NIPL, and the regulatory and operational framework of NPCI.
rationale: UPI and NPCI are highly examinable core economy topics for UPSC, especially with recent international expansions and transaction limit changes.
sectionExcerpt: Namibia became the first African country to adopt UPI. Launched by NPCI in 2016 over IMPS infrastructure, UPI allows seamless fund routing. NPCI raised per-UPI transaction limits to Rs 5 Lakh. NIPL (incorporated 2020) deploys UPI/RuPay abroad in countries like Bhutan, France, UAE, etc. NPCI is a not-for-profit initiative of RBI and IBA under the PSS Act 2007, overseeing retail payments including RuPay, NETC, and NACH.
evidenceBullets: Namibia is the first African country to adopt the UPI system for real-time digital payments.
UPI is built over the Immediate Payment Service (IMPS) infrastructure.
NPCI raised the per-UPI transaction limit to Rs 5 Lakh and the cumulative 24-hour limit to Rs 10 Lakh for Person to Merchant transactions.
NIPL is a wholly owned subsidiary of NPCI incorporated in 2020 to deploy RuPay and UPI outside India.
NPCI is a not-for-profit company initiated by RBI and IBA under the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007.

title: Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE)
focus: Historical significance, milestones, and regulatory oversight of the Bombay Stock Exchange.
rationale: BSE's 150th anniversary makes it a potential target for historical and economy-based objective questions.
sectionExcerpt: The year 2025 marks 150 years of the BSE. Established in 1875 as 'The Native Share & Stock Brokers' Association', it is Asia's first and the world's fastest Stock Exchange. In 2017, it became India's first listed stock exchange. It is regulated by SEBI.
evidenceBullets: BSE was established in 1875 as 'The Native Share & Stock Brokers' Association'.
It is Asia's first and the world's fastest Stock Exchange.
BSE became the first listed stock exchange of India in 2017.
BSE is regulated by the Securities Exchange Board of India (SEBI).

title: Sovereign Credit Rating (SCR)
focus: India's recent rating upgrades, major rating agencies, and the distinction between investment and speculative grades.
rationale: Sovereign Credit Ratings impact macroeconomic stability and foreign investment, making it a crucial concept for UPSC.
sectionExcerpt: S&P upgraded India's long-term Sovereign Credit Rating to 'BBB' and short-term to 'A-2' with a Stable Outlook. SCR evaluates a country's creditworthiness. Major agencies include S&P, Fitch, and Moody's. Ratings are broadly investment grade (BBB- to AAA for S&P/Fitch) or speculative grade. Favorable ratings facilitate global capital market access and reduce borrowing costs.
evidenceBullets: S&P upgraded India's long-term Sovereign Credit Rating to 'BBB' with a Stable Outlook.
Major SCR agencies include S&P, Fitch, and Moody's.
Investment grade ratings range from BBB- to AAA for S&P and Fitch, and Baa3 to Aaa for Moody's.
Favorable SCRs reduce borrowing costs and facilitate access to global capital markets and foreign investment.
